title: Representations of p-adic groups and Hecke algebras
speaker: Jessica Fintzen

proof_stages.label: Smooth p-adic representations and supercuspidal reduction
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a connected reductive group over a nonarchimedean local field and explains that every irreducible smooth complex representation is obtained from supercuspidal data by parabolic induction, making construction of supercuspidals the first problem.

proof_stages.label: Bernstein decomposition into inertial blocks
proof_stages.summary: Bernstein’s decomposition is introduced: the category of smooth representations splits into blocks indexed by inertial equivalence classes of Levi subgroups with supercuspidal representations, so the second problem is to understand each block.

proof_stages.label: Depth and exhaustive supercuspidal constructions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker reviews the construction history, from GL_n and classical groups through Moy-Prasad depth and Yu/Kim constructions, culminating in exhaustivity under the assumption that p does not divide the Weyl-group order, with comments on small-prime extensions.

proof_stages.label: Compact-induction realization of constructed supercuspidals
proof_stages.summary: Constructed supercuspidal representations are described as compact inductions from compact-mod-center open subgroups, with depth-zero cases coming from cuspidal representations of finite reductive quotients and positive-depth cases using deeper Moy-Prasad filtration subgroups.

proof_stages.label: Types and Hecke algebras for Bernstein blocks
proof_stages.summary: A type (K,ρ) is defined as a compact-open test representation detecting membership in a Bernstein block, and the Bushnell-Kutzko theorem identifies that block with modules over the associated convolution Hecke algebra H(G,K,ρ).

proof_stages.label: Explicit depth-zero Hecke algebras
proof_stages.summary: For depth-zero Bernstein blocks, Morris’s work and its generalizations give explicit descriptions of the Hecke algebra in terms of a finite/twisted group-algebra part and an affine Hecke algebra with parameters.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of arbitrary blocks to depth zero
proof_stages.summary: The main joint-work theorem/dream states that for any Kim-Yu type one constructs twisted-Levi depth-zero data (G^0,M^0,σ^0,K^0,ρ^0) giving an explicit isomorphism of Hecke algebras and hence an equivalence of the original Bernstein block with a depth-zero block; the corrected quadratic-character twist is essential for the statement.

examples.name: Classical and inner-form p-adic groups
examples.note: GL_n, SL_n, orthogonal groups, and units in a division algebra are offered as familiar instances of connected reductive groups over the local field, making the general setup concrete.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker cites classical groups and inner forms such as GL_N, SL_N, symplectic/orthogonal groups, and division-algebra units as motivating classes, but does not carry out a computation or construction on them.

passes.reason: Classical groups and inner forms are systematic families of reductive p-adic groups used as ordinary motivating examples.
Standard infinite families of connected reductive p-adic groups are used as ordinary motivating cases, not sporadic or boundary objects.
Standard classical p-adic groups and inner forms of GL_N are systematic ambient families, used only as representative examples.
standard systematic families of reductive p-adic groups used as motivating cases
Classical groups and inner forms are systematic infinite families used as standard reductive p-adic examples.
Classical groups and inner forms of GL_N are standard infinite-family reductive p-adic groups used as ordinary examples.
Classical groups and inner forms of GL_N are standard systematic families used as ordinary examples of reductive p-adic groups.
Systematic classical groups and inner forms used as ambient examples, not sporadic or boundary cases
Classical groups and inner forms are standard systematic families of reductive p-adic groups used only as representative examples.
Classical groups and inner forms are systematic standard families used only as ambient examples.
standard infinite classical and inner-form families of reductive p-adic groups used as motivating examples
Classical groups and inner forms are standard systematic families of p-adic reductive groups used as ordinary examples.

examples.name: SL_2 running example: supercuspidal and principal blocks
examples.note: For G=SL_2 the speaker contrasts the simple block with M=G and the principal block from the torus/trivial character, later identifying the corresponding scalar and Iwahori-Hecke algebras.
examples.engagement_reason: For G=SL_2 she analyzes the two Bernstein-block cases, describing the supercuspidal block as sums of sigma and the torus/trivial-character case via parabolic induction with trivial and Steinberg constituents, later identifying the Iwahori Hecke algebra presentation.

passes.reason: The SL_2 example uses the two extreme Levi choices, the whole group for a supercuspidal block and the torus for the principal block.
The SL_2 Bernstein-block example is a rank-one minimal case with only the top and bottom Levi possibilities and a trivial supercuspidal block.
SL_2 is the rank-one/minimal non-torus test case with only T and G as Levi choices, and the supercuspidal/principal cases are endpoint Bernstein-block examples.
rank-one SL_2 leaves only the maximal Levi M=G and minimal torus Levi M=T block cases
The SL2 running case is rank-one and displays endpoint Bernstein cases, with the supercuspidal block collapsing to the Hecke-algebra-C case.
SL_2 is used as the rank-one base case, with only the endpoint Levi cases M=G and M=T appearing.
SL_2 is used illustratively as the simplest rank-one nonabelian reductive case, with only the extreme Levi possibilities G and a torus.
Rank-one SL2 leaves only the full-group and torus Levi endpoints, with the supercuspidal block collapsing to direct sums of one irreducible
The SL2 example is rank-one with only the extreme Levi choices, and its supercuspidal block collapses to sums of one representation.
The SL2 illustration uses the rank-one extreme Levi cases M=G and M=T, with the supercuspidal block collapsing to direct sums of one representation.
the rank-one SL_2 example uses boundary Levi cases, with the M=G supercuspidal block collapsing to the simplest one-simple-object block
The SL_2 running case is the rank-one minimal setting and the blocks shown come from the endpoint Levi choices m=G and m=T.

examples.name: Depth-zero compact induction for SL_2
examples.note: The quotient SL_2(O)/congruence subgroup ≅ SL_2(k) and a cuspidal representation of this finite group illustrate how depth-zero supercuspidals arise by compact induction.
examples.engagement_reason: She explicitly runs the depth-zero construction for SL_2 by taking SL_2(O), quotienting by the congruence subgroup to get SL_2(F_q), choosing a cuspidal representation rho, and compactly inducing it to SL_2(F).

passes.reason: Depth-zero compact induction is the minimal-depth/base case and factors through the finite reductive quotient SL_2(k).
Depth zero is the minimal Moy-Prasad depth and factors through the finite quotient SL_2(k), making it a less-ramified boundary case.
The depth-zero compact induction factors through the finite quotient SL_2(k) and has the minimal possible Moy-Prasad depth.
depth zero is the minimal-depth boundary case and factors through a finite reductive quotient
Depth zero is the minimal Moy-Prasad depth and factors through a finite reductive quotient, making it the base boundary case.
Depth-zero compact induction factors through the finite reductive quotient and lies at the minimum possible Moy-Prasad depth.
The depth-zero compact induction is the minimum-depth boundary case and factors through a finite reductive quotient after killing the pro-p radical.
Depth zero is the minimal-depth boundary and the construction factors through the finite reductive quotient SL2(F_q)
Depth zero is the minimum Moy-Prasad depth and factors through a finite reductive quotient before compact induction.
The depth-zero compact induction is the minimum-depth boundary case and factors through the finite reductive quotient SL2(F_q).
the compact induction is a depth-zero construction, i.e. at the minimum possible Moy-Prasad depth
Depth-zero compact induction is the minimal-depth boundary case, factoring through the finite reductive quotient of a compact subgroup.

examples.name: Possible G^0 for SL_2 in the reduction theorem
examples.note: In response to a question, the speaker says that for SL_2 the group G^0 may be SL_2 itself in depth zero or a split or anisotropic torus in positive depth, illustrating the abstract twisted-Levi datum.
examples.engagement_reason: In the Q&A she applies the reduction setup to SL_2 and identifies the possible G^0 cases—SL_2 in depth zero or split/anisotropic tori in positive depth—with conditions on rho.

passes.reason: For SL_2 the possible G^0 collapses to either the whole group or a torus, with the torus case a minimal twisted Levi with no root structure.
For SL_2 the possible G^0 collapses to either G itself or a split/anisotropic torus, the top or minimal Levi boundary cases.
For SL_2 the possible positive-depth G^0 collapses to a torus, the rootless minimal twisted-Levi case.

For positive-depth SL_2 data the proper G^0 is forced to be a torus, a minimal rootless twisted Levi at the boundary of the Levi possibilities.

examples.name: GL_N shape of G^0 and M^0
examples.note: A final question asks for the GL_N version, and the answer sketches G^0 as a product of general linear groups over field extensions and M^0 as a subgroup with matching reductive quotient at a building point.
examples.engagement_reason: For GL_N she only sketches that G^0 is a product involving field factors and M^0 is a subgroup with matching reductive quotients, without deriving or working through the construction.

passes.reason: For GL_N the described G^0 and M^0 are standard product-of-general-linear-groups twisted Levi data in an infinite family.
